title: Price-Fairness Psychology

content.statement: People accept prices that feel fair, even if they are high.
content.question: Does your business's pricing feel fair, or merely "correct"? What would make it feel more fair?
content.equation: PFP = (Value + Transparency + Norm Consistency + Satisfaction) ÷ Price Shock
sop: Rate your pricing 1-10 on perceived Value, does the price feel earned by what's delivered (see calculation, page 5).
Rate it 1-10 on Transparency, do customers understand why it costs what it costs (see calculation, page 5).
Rate it 1-10 on Norm Consistency, does it match what similar businesses charge for similar things (see calculation, page 5).
Rate it 1-10 on Satisfaction after purchase, and rate Price Shock 1-10 for how jarring the number feels the first time someone sees it (see calculation, page 5).
Add the first three scores, divide by Price Shock, and if the score is low, add context or explanation at the point of purchase rather than just lowering the price.
content.explainer: Fair is not the same as cheap. People pay premium for "fair." They churn on "unfair" at any price.
questions: Does your business's pricing feel fair, or merely "correct"? What would make it feel more fair?
If you explained exactly why your price is what it is, would customers nod along or feel like something's being hidden from them?
Is your pricing more likely to lose you a sale, or lose you a customer's trust after they've already paid?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Value 8, Transparency 7, Norm Consistency 6, Satisfaction 8. 8 plus 7 plus 6 plus 8 equals 29, divided by Price Shock 3 equals 9.7. Higher scores mean the price feels justified even when it's high. Lower scores mean people are paying but quietly resenting it, which shows up later as churn or bad reviews.
content.closing_statement: Perceived fairness protects a price more than being cheap.

caption:
Principle Nº 63: Price-Fairness Psychology

People accept prices that feel fair, even if they are high.

Ask yourself: Does your business's pricing feel fair, or merely "correct"? What would make it feel more fair?
